candidate_problems.text: What is the actual selection mechanism that makes mathematically developed structures disproportionately align with physically instantiated ones, rather than leaving most mathematics permanently irrelevant to nature? The conjecture appeals to shared selection for invariance and compressibility, but it remains open how strong this overlap should be, what would bound it, and whether it predicts the observed frequency and depth of successful transfer from pure mathematics to physics better than rival explanations.

Can the public-good/incentive account be made quantitatively explanatory rather than metaphorical? The conjecture says mathematics is underprovided by local incentives yet still accumulates through prestige, patronage, teaching, and curiosity, but it leaves unresolved which institutional feedback loops actually generate high-generative-reach mathematics, how much underprovision to expect, and whether different incentive structures would measurably change the stock of later physically useful mathematics.

What principled criterion distinguishes a genuinely 'load-bearing' abstract structure from mathematics that is internally rich but unlikely to ever constrain empirical reality? The conjecture relies on the idea that some mathematics captures real abstract relations reusable across domains, but it does not yet explain how to identify such structures in advance, whether this can be done non-circularly, or whether the distinction is only visible retrospectively after applications appear.
